science.title: India is losing its celestial heritage. The stars in our skies are fading fast
science.description: India’s dark sky reserves at Hanle and Pench are working. The science is intact, the wildlife is recovering, and the Milky Way is still visible. But with no national policy on light pollution, everything beyond the reserve boundary is slowly disappearing into the glow.

science.title: Moon phase today: What the Moon will look like on March 28
Scientists say we’ve been looking in the wrong place for human origins
science.description: A fossil ape discovered in northern Egypt is reshaping the story of human evolution. The species, Masripithecus, lived about 17 to 18 million years ago and may sit very close to the ancestor of all modern apes. This finding challenges the long-standing focus on East Africa. Instead, it points to northern Africa and nearby regions as a possible birthplace of apes.

science.description: A 20-year cloning experiment proves that infinite asexual reproduction is a genetic dead end.

science.title: New research links cooking methods to better nutrient absorption
science.description: Researchers at the University of Seville's Food Colour and Quality Laboratory have studied the effects of different cooking methods used for tomatoes and carrots (in the oven, microwave or air fryer, amongst others) on the amount of carotenoids that are potentially available for absorption by the body following the digestion of these foods.

ai.title: Why AI that says ‘you’re right’ could be dangerous, according to a new study
ai.description: A new study has found that AI chatbots that excessively agree with users, known as ‘sycophantic AI’, can have negative effects. These systems may distort judgement, reduce accountability, and reinforce harmful beliefs. Despite this, users still prefer sycophantic AI and its impact persists. The researchers suggest prioritising user well-being and educating them about AI’s limitations.
